quiz_questions.question_text: Which fossil fuel produces the most CO2 per unit of energy when burned?

options.text: Natural gas
Oil
Propane
Coal

quiz_questions.explanation: Coal produces approximately twice as much CO2 per unit of energy as natural gas and about 30% more than oil, making it the most carbon-intensive fossil fuel.

quiz_questions.question_text: The three main fossil fuels are:

options.text: Coal, petroleum (oil), and natural gas
Uranium, plutonium, and thorium
Wind, solar, and geothermal
Wood, peat, and charcoal

quiz_questions.explanation: Coal, petroleum, and natural gas are the three primary fossil fuels, all formed from ancient organic matter over millions of years and currently providing about 80% of global energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Natural gas is considered the cleanest fossil fuel because it:

options.text: Produces no emissions at all
Produces roughly half the CO2 of coal per unit of energy and fewer air pollutants
Is a renewable resource
Does not contribute to climate change

quiz_questions.explanation: Natural gas produces about 50-60% less CO2 than coal and significantly fewer pollutants like SO2 and particulates, though methane leaks during extraction and transport can offset some climate benefits.

quiz_questions.question_text: Hydraulic fracturing (fracking) extracts natural gas by:

options.text: Injecting high-pressure water, sand, and chemicals into rock to crack it and release trapped gas
Heating underground rock with lasers
Mining underground tunnels
Using bacteria to break down rock

quiz_questions.explanation: Fracking injects millions of gallons of pressurized water mixed with sand and chemicals deep underground to fracture shale rock formations, releasing natural gas that was previously inaccessible.

quiz_questions.question_text: The largest proven oil reserves in the world are located in:

options.text: Australia and New Zealand
Venezuela and Saudi Arabia
The United States
China and India

quiz_questions.explanation: Venezuela holds the world's largest proven oil reserves (approximately 300 billion barrels), followed closely by Saudi Arabia, though extraction costs and oil quality vary significantly.

quiz_questions.question_text: Coal mining poses health risks to miners including:

options.text: Improved lung capacity
Black lung disease (pneumoconiosis) from prolonged inhalation of coal dust
Better cardiovascular health
No known health effects

quiz_questions.explanation: Coal miners face serious health risks including black lung disease caused by years of breathing coal dust that scars lung tissue, as well as risks from mine collapses, explosions, and toxic gas exposure.

quiz_questions.question_text: Petroleum is refined into many products including:

options.text: Gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, plastics, pharmaceuticals, and fertilizers
Only gasoline
Only lubricants
Only heating oil

quiz_questions.explanation: Petroleum is remarkably versatile, serving as feedstock for thousands of products beyond fuel, including plastics, synthetic fabrics, medicines, and agricultural chemicals, making it deeply embedded in modern life.

quiz_questions.question_text: Mountaintop removal mining for coal involves:

options.text: Using solar-powered machinery to mine gently
Blasting away mountain tops to expose coal seams, dumping debris into adjacent valleys
Carefully extracting coal with minimal disruption
Tunneling under mountains without surface disturbance

quiz_questions.explanation: Mountaintop removal permanently destroys mountain ecosystems, buries streams under rubble, contaminates waterways with toxic runoff, and has devastated large areas of Appalachia.

quiz_questions.question_text: Fossil fuels currently provide approximately what percentage of global energy?

options.text: About 95%
About 50%
About 20%
About 80%

quiz_questions.explanation: Despite renewable energy growth, fossil fuels still supply approximately 80% of global primary energy, highlighting the enormous scale of the transition needed to decarbonize the world energy system.

quiz_questions.question_text: Tar sands extraction is controversial because it:

options.text: Requires massive energy input, large land disturbance, and produces significantly more greenhouse gases than conventional oil
Only exists in Antarctica
Produces no pollution
Is the cheapest form of oil production

quiz_questions.explanation: Tar sands extraction requires strip mining or steam injection, uses enormous amounts of water and energy, and produces 17% more greenhouse gases per barrel than conventional oil.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a fossil fuel?

options.text: Any fuel that produces fire
A fuel made from modern plant material
A hydrocarbon-based fuel formed from remains of ancient organisms subjected to heat and pressure over millions of years
Any fuel that comes from the ground

quiz_questions.explanation: Fossil fuels are energy-rich hydrocarbons originating from buried remains of prehistoric plants and marine organisms, transformed by geological heat and pressure over tens to hundreds of millions of years.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is acid mine drainage?

options.text: Acidic water produced when exposed sulfide minerals in mine waste react with air and water, contaminating streams with heavy metals
Clean water flowing from mine exits
A technique for improving water quality near mines
Water used to wash coal before burning

quiz_questions.explanation: Acid mine drainage occurs when sulfur-bearing minerals exposed by mining oxidize to form sulfuric acid, which leaches toxic heavy metals from rock and contaminates surrounding waterways for decades.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is methane and why is it significant as a greenhouse gas?

options.text: Methane (CH4) is the primary component of natural gas and traps about 80 times more heat than CO2 over a 20-year period
Methane has no greenhouse effect
Methane is a type of coal
Methane is CO2 and has no special properties

quiz_questions.explanation: Methane is a potent greenhouse gas with approximately 80 times the warming potential of CO2 over 20 years, and significant quantities leak from natural gas wells, pipelines, and coal mines.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is carbon capture and storage (CCS)?

options.text: Storing coal in underground bunkers
Technology that captures CO2 emissions from power plants and stores them underground in geological formations to prevent atmospheric release
Planting trees to absorb CO2
Burning more fossil fuels to trap carbon

quiz_questions.explanation: CCS captures CO2 at the point of emission and injects it into deep geological formations for permanent storage, though it remains expensive and not yet deployed at the scale needed for significant emission reduction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is EROI (Energy Return on Investment)?

options.text: The financial cost of energy production
The amount of energy stored in batteries
The environmental rating of energy sources
The ratio of usable energy produced to the energy invested to obtain it, where higher EROI means more net energy gained

quiz_questions.explanation: EROI measures net energy: conventional oil has an EROI of about 15:1, while tar sands may be as low as 3:1, meaning increasingly difficult-to-access fossil fuels yield less net energy per unit invested.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is petroleum?

options.text: A liquid mixture of hydrocarbons found in underground rock formations, refined into fuels and products like plastics
A gas used only for heating homes
A solid mineral mined from mountains
A renewable liquid fuel from crops

quiz_questions.explanation: Petroleum, or crude oil, is a complex mixture of hundreds of different hydrocarbons found in sedimentary rock formations, processed in refineries into transportation fuels, heating oil, and thousands of petrochemical products.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the greenhouse effect?

options.text: The warming inside an actual greenhouse
A cooling effect caused by air pollution
The process by which certain atmospheric gases trap outgoing infrared radiation, warming Earth's surface above what it would be without an atmosphere
The effect of ozone depletion on plant growth

quiz_questions.explanation: The greenhouse effect is a natural process where gases like CO2, methane, and water vapor absorb and re-emit infrared radiation, but human emissions of these gases are intensifying the effect and causing global warming.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a carbon tax?

options.text: A tax refund for using fossil fuels
A tax on all carbon-based products
A fee on fossil fuel carbon content designed to make environmental costs visible in market prices and incentivize cleaner alternatives
A tax on carbon fiber materials

quiz_questions.explanation: A carbon tax charges fossil fuel producers or users based on the CO2 their fuel generates, internalizing the external cost of climate change and creating economic incentives for energy efficiency and renewable energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is strip mining?

options.text: Extracting oil from the ocean floor
Mining along coastlines only
A surface mining method that removes overlying soil and rock to expose and extract shallow mineral or coal deposits
Mining through vertical shafts deep underground

quiz_questions.explanation: Strip mining removes vegetation, soil, and rock in strips to access shallow coal or mineral seams, causing severe land disturbance, habitat destruction, and water contamination if not properly reclaimed.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is cogeneration (combined heat and power)?

options.text: Running two separate power plants side by side
A system that simultaneously produces electricity and captures waste heat for useful purposes, achieving 60-80% total efficiency
Mixing coal and natural gas before burning
Generating electricity from two different fuels simultaneously

quiz_questions.explanation: Cogeneration captures waste heat from power generation for building heating or industrial processes, raising overall energy efficiency from roughly 33% to 60-80% and reducing total fuel consumption.

quiz_questions.question_text: A coal-fired power plant in the Midwest emits SO2 and NOx that form acid rain, damaging lakes in northeastern states and Canada. This situation illustrates that:

options.text: Coal combustion produces no air pollutants
Air pollution always stays near its source
Fossil fuel pollution can travel hundreds of miles, causing transboundary environmental damage far from the emission source
Acid rain only affects the area near the plant

quiz_questions.explanation: Prevailing winds can transport SO2 and NOx emissions hundreds of miles from coal plants, creating acid deposition that damages ecosystems in regions far from the pollution source.

quiz_questions.question_text: A community near a fracking operation notices their well water can be set on fire with a lighter. Testing reveals methane contamination. This is most likely caused by:

options.text: Natural methane that always exists in well water
Methane migrating into groundwater through cracks created by fracking or poorly sealed well casings
Normal chemical reactions in water heaters
The homeowner adding methane to their water

quiz_questions.explanation: Fracking can allow methane to migrate into drinking water aquifers through fractures in rock or improperly cemented well casings, contaminating groundwater supplies and creating explosion hazards.

quiz_questions.question_text: China burns more coal than any other country, causing severe urban air pollution. The government has begun closing coal plants and investing in renewables. This transition is challenging because:

options.text: Coal produces no pollution in China
Renewable energy is always more expensive than coal in China
China has no renewable energy potential
Coal provides cheap, reliable baseload power, and transitioning requires massive investment while maintaining energy security for 1.4 billion people

quiz_questions.explanation: China's coal transition illustrates the tension between cheap fossil fuel energy for development and health/environmental consequences, requiring enormous investment to replace coal while maintaining energy access.

quiz_questions.question_text: An oil company discovers a large offshore deposit in critical whale habitat. The government must decide whether to allow drilling. An environmental scientist would recommend:

options.text: Ban all offshore drilling everywhere
Conduct a comprehensive environmental impact assessment evaluating spill risks, noise impacts on whales, and comparing economic benefits against ecological costs
Drill immediately without assessment
Whales will not be affected by drilling

quiz_questions.explanation: A thorough EIA would evaluate spill risks, seismic survey impacts on whale communication, and ecosystem disruption against economic benefits, potentially identifying mitigation measures or alternative sites.

quiz_questions.question_text: A natural gas pipeline develops a small leak undetected for months. This is environmentally significant because:

options.text: Only large leaks matter for climate change
Natural gas leaks have no environmental impact
The leaked gas is naturally absorbed by soil
Methane is a potent greenhouse gas, and cumulative small leaks across gas infrastructure can significantly offset climate benefits of switching from coal to gas

quiz_questions.explanation: If methane leakage rates exceed about 3% of total production, natural gas loses its climate advantage over coal, and cumulative small leaks throughout the supply chain can reach or exceed this threshold.

quiz_questions.question_text: A developing nation discovers significant natural gas reserves and debates whether to develop them or leapfrog directly to renewables. The strongest argument for the renewable path is:

options.text: Investing in long-lived gas infrastructure creates carbon lock-in for decades, while renewable costs are declining rapidly
Natural gas has no economic value
Natural gas is always more expensive than renewables
Renewable energy cannot power an economy

quiz_questions.explanation: Building fossil fuel infrastructure with 30-50 year lifespans locks in carbon emissions for decades, while rapidly falling renewable energy costs mean investing in clean energy now may be more economical long-term.

quiz_questions.question_text: A homeowner near a fracking site sees their property value drop 25%, while the gas company creates 200 local jobs and pays royalties to some landowners. This demonstrates:

options.text: Property values always increase near gas wells
The uneven distribution of costs and benefits from fossil fuel extraction, where some benefit while others bear disproportionate burdens
Fracking benefits everyone equally
Environmental concerns are always exaggerated

quiz_questions.explanation: Fossil fuel extraction often creates winners and losers within the same community, with some benefiting from jobs and royalties while nearby residents suffer from noise, pollution risks, and declining property values.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ice core analysis shows CO2 remained between 180-280 ppm for 800,000 years before rising to over 420 ppm since the Industrial Revolution. The primary cause is:

options.text: Natural volcanic activity
Changes in ocean circulation
Increased plant decomposition
Massive combustion of fossil fuels since the Industrial Revolution, releasing carbon stored underground for millions of years

quiz_questions.explanation: By burning fossil fuels, humans have transferred vast quantities of carbon from geological storage into the atmosphere in just 200 years, raising CO2 concentrations 50% above pre-industrial levels at an unprecedented rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: An island nation dependent on imported diesel for electricity faces costs three times the global average. Solar panels and batteries could reduce costs by 60%. The primary barrier is:

options.text: Islands receive no sunlight
Diesel is always cheaper than solar everywhere
The high upfront capital cost of solar and battery systems, even though long-term savings would be substantial
Solar energy does not work on islands

quiz_questions.explanation: Many island nations remain locked into expensive diesel generation because they lack access to upfront capital for renewable energy systems, despite the technology being far cheaper over its lifetime.

quiz_questions.question_text: For the AP exam, fossil fuel questions require students to connect energy production to multiple environmental issues including:

options.text: Climate change, air and water pollution, habitat destruction, human health impacts, environmental justice, and policy trade-offs
Only the cost of gasoline
Only combustion chemistry
Only air pollution

quiz_questions.explanation: AP Environmental Science requires analyzing fossil fuels holistically, connecting extraction impacts, combustion emissions, health effects, environmental justice concerns, and policy responses across multiple systems.
